FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, baseline ages 9-10, longitudinal design with EARS passive sensing substudy

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-sectional passive-sensed smartphone use data from ABCD Release 5.0, validated against self-report

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Scientific Reports 2024

FT_IC2.evidence: Pure psychometric/validation paper. Measures phone-specific estimates (keystroke count: r=0.33 with app use; correlations 0.21-0.35 with self-report; mean 5 h/day smartphone use, females > males) but paired only with descriptive demographics (sex, income, education, race) and validation metrics. No individual-trait-level outcome variable (neural, behavioral, health, cognitive, psychiatric, sleep, academic, personality, etc.). Paper analyzes smartphone use measurement properties and trends, not associations between SM/phone use and within-person constructs.

notes: FT-IC2 NOT_MET triggers cascade; all downstream DEFERRED. Paper validates EARS passive-sensing measures against self-report and describes smartphone use patterns by sex and OS, but does not analyze associations with individual-trait variables.

FT_IC1.evidence: Describes ABCD Study cohort (11,876 youth, 21 sites, ages 9-10 baseline); resource is dedicated to ABCD research community

FT_IC5.evidence: Non-empirical resource/methods paper. Describes development of NowIKnowMyABCD—a documentation website, discussion board, and Twitter feed for ABCD researchers. No original empirical analysis of ABCD participant data. Paper is a resource description, not a research study.

notes: FT-IC5 NOT_MET (non-empirical) triggers cascade. This is a resource/infrastructure paper, not a research article with original empirical analyses of ABCD data.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, N=11,868 baseline, ages ~9/10 to ~13/14 across waves 0-4; 21 U.S. sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: descriptive statistics and multilevel logistic regression models of youth self-reported technology use (6 subtypes: TV/movies, video games, YouTube, social media, video chat, texting) across longitudinal waves

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Adolescent Health 2024

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Identifies phone/SM-specific variables with numeric estimates. Example: multilevel logistic regression OR for study wave (continuous): social media OR=3.34 (95% CI 3.14-3.55), texting OR=2.22 (2.07-2.39), video chat OR=1.79 (1.67-1.93), youtube OR=1.25 (1.16-1.35), video games OR=2.11 (2.04-2.18), TV OR=2.16 (2.09-2.23). Step 1 qualifies. BUT other-side rule fails: ALL analyses pair technology variables exclusively with demographic covariates only—biological sex, parent education (categorical: no college, Bachelor's, Master's, Doctoral/Professional), parent combined income (6 categories), and parent marital status (6 categories). No individual-trait-level outcome on other side (no neural, behavioral, health, cognitive, psychiatric, sleep, academic, personality, substance use, etc.). Demographic-only on other side triggers FT-IC2 NOT_MET per binding rule.

notes: FT-IC2 NOT_MET (demographic-only on other side) triggers cascade. Paper analyzes associations between technology subtypes and sociodemographic predictors, but lacks individual-trait-level outcomes (behavioral health, mental health, cognitive, academic, personality, etc.).
